citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two dogs were observed to have dental issues. A 2 year old female Poodle named "Adaline" (#102) had a moderate
amount of tartar buildup on both sides of the mouth. In addition, the gums were red and the gumline was starting to
recede in the back of the mouth on both sides.
A 5-6 year old male Poodle named "Galen" (#087) had a significant amount of tartar buildup with reddened gums.
Dental issues can be painful and can become infected. The licensee must consult with the attending veterinarian and treat
both dogs per his/her instructions. Going forward, the licensee must observe all dogs on a daily basis to ensure dental
and other veterinary care issues are observed and addressed by the attending veterinarian. Correct by 2/27/26.

A 4.5 month old dog had no identification. Not having proper identification on all dogs doesn't allow for APHIS officials to
properly track the movement of the dogs through the kennel. The licensee must identify all dogs in the kennel in
accordance with the Animal Welfare Act. Correct starting on 3/3/26.

One enclosure containing a mom and six 3-week old puppies did not have identification for the puppies. Not having
proper identification on all puppies doesn't allow for APHIS officials to properly track the movement of the dogs through
the kennel. The licensee must identify all puppies in the kennel in accordance with the Animal Welfare Act. Correct
starting on 3/3/26.

Two enclosures (containing a total of two dogs) had nail heads sticking out of the wall on the outdoor parts of the
enclosures. The nail heads were located at the level of the dogs. Nail head sticking out could injure the dogs as they walk
past the wall. The enclosures must be designed so they are structurally sound and kept in good repair. They must protect
the animals from injury. The licensee must fix the enclosures so nail heads are no longer sticking out into the enclosures.
Correct by 3/3/26.

At least two feeders (accessed by 8 dogs) were contaminated with wood shavings. Contaminated feed containers could
cause the dogs to become sick. The licensee must ensure the feed containers don't become contaminated with wood
shavings. Correct starting on 2/25/26.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The outdoor part of the sheltered enclosures hadn't been sanitized in a month. The grates the dogs walk on is coated with
fecal material that has built up over the past month. The outside part of the door frames of the doggy doors had a buildup
of dirt and grim. The feeders and watering source for the dogs does not get sanitized every two weeks as it should. Not
sanitizing the enclosures, feeders, and watering source on a biweekly basis allows for fecal material and grime to build up
which can contribute to the spread of disease. The licensee must sanitize the enclosures, feeders, and watering source
every two weeks to prevent the accumulation of dirt, debris, excreta, and other disease hazards. Correct by 3/3/26.

citations.desc: Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control.
citations.narrative:
The food storage area had clutter laying around. In addition mouse feces was present throughout the food storage area
and sand had blown in from outside and covered the floor of the food storage area. Not keeping the food storage area
clean doesn't facilitate good husbandry practices and can contribute to the spread of disease. The licensee must clean up
the food storage area to eliminate the clutter, sand that has built up, and the mouse feces that has accumulated. Correct
by 3/3/26.

The licensee is not following the vaccination and deworming schedule that the attending veterinarian has written up in the
program of veterinary care. Not following the program of veterinary care doesn't ensure the dogs are adequately protected
from the various diseases and parasites that these vaccines and dewormers are meant to protect against. The licensee
must start following the instructions as written in the program of veterinary care. Correct starting on 2/25/26.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative.
End of report.
Additional Inspectors:
KELLY MAXWELL n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two dogs were observed to have dental issues. A 2 year old female Poodle named "Adaline" (#102) had a moderate 
amount of tartar buildup on both sides of the mouth. In addition, the gums were red and the gumline was starting to 
recede in the back of the mouth on both sides. 
 
A 5-6 year old male Poodle named "Galen" (#087) had a significant amount of tartar buildup with reddened gums. 
 
Dental issues can be painful and can become infected. The licensee must consult with the attending veterinarian and treat 
both dogs per his/her instructions. Going forward, the licensee must observe all dogs on a daily basis to ensure dental 
and other veterinary care issues are observed and addressed by the attending veterinarian. Correct by 2/27/26. 
2.50(a)(1) 
   
Time and method of identification. 
A 4.5 month old dog had no identification. Not having proper identification on all dogs doesn't allow for APHIS officials to 
properly track the movement of the dogs through the kennel. The licensee must identify all dogs in the kennel in 
accordance with the Animal Welfare Act. Correct starting on 3/3/26. 
2.50(a)(2) 
   
Time and method of identification. 
One enclosure containing a mom and six 3-week old puppies did not have identification for the puppies. Not having 
proper identification on all puppies doesn't allow for APHIS officials to properly track the movement of the dogs through 
the kennel. The licensee must identify all puppies in the kennel in accordance with the Animal Welfare Act. Correct 
starting on 3/3/26. 
3.1(a) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
Two enclosures (containing a total of two dogs) had nail heads sticking out of the wall on the outdoor parts of the 
enclosures. The nail heads were located at the level of the dogs. Nail head sticking out could injure the dogs as they walk 
past the wall. The enclosures must be designed so they are structurally sound and kept in good repair. They must protect

the animals from injury. The licensee must fix the enclosures so nail heads are no longer sticking out into the enclosures. 
Correct by 3/3/26. 
3.9(b) 
   
Feeding 
At least two feeders (accessed by 8 dogs) were contaminated with wood shavings. Contaminated feed containers could 
cause the dogs to become sick. The licensee must ensure the feed containers don't become contaminated with wood 
shavings. Correct starting on 2/25/26. 
3.11(b)(2) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The outdoor part of the sheltered enclosures hadn't been sanitized in a month. The grates the dogs walk on is coated with 
fecal material that has built up over the past month. The outside part of the door frames of the doggy doors had a buildup 
of dirt and grim. The feeders and watering source for the dogs does not get sanitized every two weeks as it should. Not 
sanitizing the enclosures, feeders, and watering source on a biweekly basis allows for fecal material and grime to build up 
which can contribute to the spread of disease. The licensee must sanitize the enclosures, feeders, and watering source 
every two weeks to prevent the accumulation of dirt, debris, excreta, and other disease hazards. Correct by 3/3/26. 
3.11(c) 
   
Cleaning, sanitization, housekeeping, and pest control. 
The food storage area had clutter laying around. In addition mouse feces was present throughout the food storage area 
and sand had blown in from outside and covered the floor of the food storage area. Not keeping the food storage area 
clean doesn't facilitate good husbandry practices and can contribute to the spread of disease. The licensee must clean up 
the food storage area to eliminate the clutter, sand that has built up, and the mouse feces that has accumulated. Correct 
by 3/3/26. 
3.13(a)(3) 
   
Veterinary care for dogs. 
The licensee is not following the vaccination and deworming schedule that the attending veterinarian has written up in the 
program of veterinary care. Not following the program of veterinary care doesn't ensure the dogs are adequately protected 
from the various diseases and parasites that these vaccines and dewormers are meant to protect against. The licensee 
must start following the instructions as written in the program of veterinary care. Correct starting on 2/25/26. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representative. 
 
End of report.

Additional Inspectors:
